name: Battlestar Galactica
description: Lines of survival, faith, and frakkin' defiance from the reimagined Battlestar Galactica.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: So say we all.
quotes.notes: Colonial call-and-response affirmation of unity. Adama first rouses the crew with it at a memorial service, and it becomes the series' signature rallying cry.

quotes.content: Are you alive?
quotes.notes: Six's first words to the human officer at the armistice station, opening the reimagined series.

quotes.content: Humanity's children are returning home... today.
quotes.notes: Six's chilling herald of the Cylon attack on the Twelve Colonies in the opening scene.

quotes.content: The Cylons were created by man. They rebelled. They evolved. They look and feel human. Some are programmed to think they are human. There are many copies. And they have a plan.
quotes.notes: The Season 1 broadcast title card (Season 2+ used a rewritten narration, and the miniseries used a different one still). The previous text in this collection dropped the 'They look and feel human. Some are programmed to think they are human' sentence, which is specific to Season 1 and ties directly to that season's sleeper-agent storyline. Creator Ronald D. Moore later admitted there was, in fact, no plan.

quotes.content: You cannot play God then wash your hands of the things that you've created. Sooner or later, the day comes when you can't hide from the things that you've done anymore.
quotes.notes: From Adama's decommissioning-ceremony speech, an unscripted reflection on humanity's responsibility for creating the Cylons.

quotes.content: Why are we as a people worth saving?
quotes.notes: The question at the heart of Adama's decommissioning speech, framing the series' central theme.

quotes.content: The cost of wearing the uniform can be high, but sometimes it's too high.
quotes.notes: Adama on the toll of military service, from the Galactica's decommissioning ceremony.

quotes.content: If we are even going to survive as a species, then we need to get the hell out of here, and we need to start having babies.
quotes.notes: Roslin convincing Adama that flight and survival, not a hopeless fight, are humanity's only path forward.

quotes.content: Nothin' but the rain, sir.
quotes.notes: Starbuck's answer to Adama's recurring pre-launch ritual, 'What do you hear, Starbuck?'

quotes.content: There's a reason you separate military and the police. One fights the enemies of the state, the other serves and protects the people. When the military becomes both, then the enemies of the state tend to become the people.
quotes.notes: Adama warning against militarizing civilian life; the line is frequently shared online, often without crediting the show.

quotes.content: Pilots call me Starbuck, you may refer to me as God.
quotes.notes: Starbuck's cocky introduction to a class of nugget trainees.

quotes.content: Cylons are, in the final analysis, little more than toasters with great-looking legs.
quotes.source: Battlestar Galactica, S1E7 'Six Degrees of Separation' (2004)
quotes.notes: Baltar leans into the fleet's 'toaster' slur for Cylons, with characteristic self-serving bravado.

quotes.content: All of this has happened before, and all of this will happen again.
quotes.notes: Leoben voicing the show's cyclical-time motif, drawn from the sacred scrolls; the line recurs throughout the series.

quotes.content: Sometimes you have to roll the hard six.
quotes.source: Battlestar Galactica, S1E10 'The Hand of God' (2005)
quotes.notes: Adama on taking the long-odds gamble; a dice-game metaphor that became one of his signature lines.

quotes.content: When you are in the cockpit, you are in control.
quotes.source: Battlestar Galactica, S1E10 'The Hand of God' (2005)
quotes.notes: Adama's advice to a pilot about command and composure under pressure.

quotes.content: God doesn't take sides. He only wants your love.
quotes.source: Battlestar Galactica, S1E10 'The Hand of God' (2005)
quotes.notes: Virtual Six pressing her monotheistic faith on Baltar.

quotes.content: Yes, we're tired. Yes, there is no relief. Yes, the Cylons keep coming after us time after time after time. And yes, we are still expected to do our jobs!
quotes.notes: Tigh rallying an exhausted crew during the relentless Cylon pursuit of the series premiere.

quotes.content: It's not enough to survive. One has to be worthy of survival.
quotes.notes: Adama on the moral line he refuses to cross even against the Cylons. Some transcripts render it 'One must be worthy of survival.'

quotes.content: I am an angel of God sent here to protect you.
quotes.notes: Virtual Six defining her role in Baltar's head as the series' religious arc deepens.

quotes.content: It's all so pointless. We kill them, and they kill us.
quotes.notes: Baltar despairing at the endless cycle of human-Cylon violence.

quotes.content: We're on the side of the demons, Chief.
quotes.notes: Tigh justifying the insurgency's brutal tactics during the Cylon occupation of New Caprica.

quotes.content: Don't make me cry on my own hangar deck.
quotes.notes: Adama's gruff, emotional aside as the fleet pulls together for the rescue from New Caprica.

quotes.content: Blood for blood has to stop one day.
quotes.notes: Baltar on the cycle of reprisals during the occupation.

quotes.content: We cannot sacrifice democracy just because the president makes a bad decision.
quotes.source: Battlestar Galactica, S1E13 'Kobol's Last Gleaming, Part 2' (2005)
quotes.notes: Apollo on the constitutional limits of military authority as Adama and Roslin clash over power.

quotes.content: You love me. You can't take it back.
quotes.notes: Starbuck to Lee, a rare unguarded moment in their long, fraught relationship.
